did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper states that 'CNN models help the Things Board IoT communication of skin lesion images within medical IoT platform automatically classify lesions as benign or malignant.' This indicates the use of Convolutional Neural Networks (a deep learning method) for the dermatology task of skin lesion classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study of a proposed IoT-enabled diagnostic device for skin lesion monitoring. It details the system design, simulation using Proteus, hardware components (Arduino UNO R3, OV7670 camera), and reports specific experimental results including 98.3% accuracy, transmission latency metrics, and performance comparisons with MQTT and CoAP protocols. Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE STI 2025), it contains original technical results and validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: IoT-Enabled Diagnostic Device for Real-Time Skin Lesion Monitoring and Detection
journal: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Sustainable Technologies for Industry 5.0 (STI 2025)
brief_summary: This study presents an IoT-enabled diagnostic device for real-time skin lesion monitoring and detection, aiming to improve early skin cancer identification. The system utilizes an OV7670 camera module and Arduino UNO R3 to capture high-resolution images, which are processed locally and transmitted securely using Base64 encoding, HL7 formatting, and RSA encryption. The architecture integrates MQTT for IoT communication and uploads data to an FTP server, with CNN models employed to classify lesions as benign or malignant. Experimental results demonstrated a 98.3% end-to-end accuracy and a 6.8-second transmission time for 5MB images. The system outperformed MQTT and CoAP protocols in latency and reliability, achieving response times under two seconds across various bandwidths, making it suitable for tele-dermatology applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: The system is designed to capture and analyze images of 'skin lesions' and 'suspicious skin areas' for early detection of skin cancer. The CNN models classify these lesions broadly as 'benign or malignant'.
Specifically mentioned as a key target for detection. The system differentiates 'moles from melanoma cells' and detects 'early melanoma and other skin malignancies'.
The system is described as differentiating 'moles from melanoma cells' and tracking changes in 'mole size, shape, and color'.

The CNN classifies lesions as 'benign or malignant'. The text notes that high-resolution output enables tracking of disorders like 'rashes, acne, eczema, psoriasis, and dermatitis', implying these are part of the benign category or conditions the system can monitor.
The simulation displays 'Warning - Abnormal' when a 'potentially cancerous or abnormal skin lesion, such as a tumor' is detected.

all_task_types.details: The paper states that CNN models are used to 'automatically classify lesions as benign or malignant.' The system differentiates between moles (benign) and melanoma cells (malignant), displaying alerts for 'abnormal' or 'potentially cancerous' lesions.

datasets_used.name: Not specified (Simulated/Prototype Images)
datasets_used.description: The study does not report using a specific public or private named dataset. Instead, it describes a prototype system where 'photographs of various body locations (locations of Interest, ROI)' are taken to test the hardware and transmission pipeline. The system is also adapted for skin cancer detection using 'skin lesion images as input' in a simulation environment (ThingsBoard, Proteus, NS3).

use.testing: Used for testing the transmission pipeline, latency, and accuracy of the IoT device and HL7 formatting. The CNN model's classification accuracy (98.3%) is reported, but the specific dataset split for this model is not detailed.
use.other: Used for simulation and prototype validation in Proteus and NS3.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reports descriptive performance metrics (e.g., 98.3% accuracy, 6.8s transmission time) and qualitative comparisons with MQTT and CoAP protocols without statistical validation.

Prototype System (Arduino UNO R3 + OV767.notes: Accuracy achieved with little errors from hardware noise or lighting changes.

HL7 Message Formation Stage.notes: Small variations due to data overflow or formatting problems.

Transmission Phase (Socket + FTP).notes: Occasionally impacted by network instability or packet loss.

End-to-End System Performance.notes: Attests to resilience and dependability for managing medical images securely.

Transmission Latency (5MB Image).notes: Acceptable for near real-time medical diagnostics. Breakdown: Image Capture (0.95s), Base64 Encoding (0.48s), HL7 Message Creation (0.32s), HL7+MLLP Socket Transmission (1.2s), FTP Upload (1.5s), MQTT Metadata Transfer (0.18s). Total end-to-end delay to Master Database: 4.3 seconds.

Network Latency (MDB to FTP).notes: Data size kept relatively constant near 9MB for MDB to IoT tests. RT decreases as BW increases.

Network Latency (MDB to IoT).notes: RT starts at 1.16s at 1 Mbps and falls to minimum 0.44s at 15 Mbps. Slight increase at 20 Mbps may indicate congestion.

Network Latency (OV7670 to MDB).notes: Demonstrates inverse relationship between bandwidth and transfer time.

Proposed Algorithm.Suitability for Medical: Optimized for large binary image data transfers
Proposed Algorithm.QoS Levels: 3 (Exactly once), 2 (At least once), 1 (At most once), 0 (Fire and Forget)

MQTT.Suitability for Medical: Not optimized for large binary image data transfers

CoAP.Suitability for Medical: Less suited for large binary image data transfers

System Comparison (Proposed vs MQTT vs C.notes: Proposed algorithm outperforms MQTT and CoAP in latency and dependability, reaching response times under two seconds spanning 2G to broadband bandwidths.

Image Display Delay (Patient Monitoring).notes: Delay decreases with higher bandwidth. Image sizes: <1MB (2G: 6s, 3G: 6s, 4G: 5s, Broadband: 5s); 1-3MB (2G: 15s, 3G: 10s, 4G: 5s, Broadband: 5s); 3-5MB (2G: 22s, 3G: 15s, 4G: 10s, Broadband: 5s); >5MB (2G: 22s, 3G: 15s, 4G: 10s, Broadband: 5s).

architecture_details.specific_architecture: Not specified (referenced as 'CNN models' and 'AI-based classification'), with ResNet mentioned as an example in the context of ThingsBoard integration

training_details.dataset: Not specified (simulated/prototype images used for system validation; specific CNN training dataset not reported)

classification_or_output_head.details: Classifies skin lesions as benign or malignant; displays 'Warning - Abnormal' for potentially cancerous lesions

preprocessing_techniques: Base64 encoding of binary image data to convert it into an ASCII string for HL7 message embedding
HL7 message formatting (embedding encoded image into OBX or custom Z-segments) for healthcare data standard compliance
RSA encryption applied to the image file or generated FTP link for secure transmission
MQTT protocol framing for IoT communication and message management
conclusions: The proposed IoT-enabled diagnostic device achieves 98.3% end-to-end accuracy for skin lesion classification.
The system utilizes Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to automatically classify skin lesions as benign or malignant within a medical IoT platform.
The system demonstrates a transmission time of 6.8 seconds for 5MB images, which is considered acceptable for near real-time medical diagnostics.
The proposed algorithm outperforms MQTT and CoAP protocols in terms of latency and dependability, achieving response times under two seconds across bandwidths ranging from 2G to broadband.
The system is optimized for large binary image data transfers by using HL7 message formatting and FTP uploads, addressing limitations of standard MQTT and CoAP for medical imaging.
The architecture supports scalable processing, capable of handling 24–26 images every 30 seconds.
The system ensures data security and integrity through Base64 encoding, HL7 formatting, and RSA encryption.
The study acknowledges that future work will focus on AI model improvement and multi-modal data integration.
important_remarks: The CNN model used for classification is not specified in detail (architecture, layers, or training dataset), and its performance (98.3% accuracy) is reported as part of the end-to-end system accuracy without independent validation metrics for the AI component.

The study relies on simulation (Proteus, NS3) and prototype testing rather than clinical trials with human subjects, limiting the generalizability of the diagnostic accuracy to real-world clinical populations.

No specific public or private dermatological dataset (e.g., ISIC, HAM10000) was used; the system was tested on photographs of various body locations taken for prototype validation, introducing potential population and dataset bias.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests, confidence intervals, or sensitivity/specificity metrics, relying instead on descriptive accuracy and latency metrics.
The hardware prototype uses an OV7670 camera (640x480 resolution) which may have lower image quality compared to clinical-grade dermatoscopes, potentially affecting diagnostic precision.
The system's performance claims (e.g., response times <2s) are based on simulated network conditions (NS3) and may not reflect real-world variability in internet connectivity and latency.
The study acknowledges that future work will focus on AI model improvement, indicating that the current AI component may be a preliminary or placeholder implementation.
